entry.title: Recognizing Educational Excellence: UCS Honored at SAP Academic Community Conference LAC 2026
entry.content:
[This blog post is the translation in LinkedIn from the original post in English by Mateus Panizzon, from University UCS, Brazil: Post | Feed | LinkedIn ]  

I’m grateful to share that UCS - University of Caxias do Sul > UCS Business School received the SAP University Alliances Excellence Award 2026/2027 (Education) at the #SAP Academic Community Conference LAC 2026.

The SAP Excellence honors outstanding lighthouse institutions in the SAP University Alliances network, in three categories: #education, #research, and #innovation.

The award process involved submitting a report on the last years of SAP University Alliances initiatives at UCS, including the adoption of SAP technologies in undergraduate, specialization, and graduate programs; participation in events; missions to SAP Experience Centers; joint publications; and industry collaboration.

The report was blind-reviewed by the Academic Board in Germany and Asia-Pacific (APJC).

This recognition is the result of the efforts of many people who bring the SAP Academic Alliance Program to life: SAP and UCS industry partners, especially, Randoncorp, who motivated us since the very beginning, several faculty managers and coordinators,  professors, and especially the students — the future talents at the center of this process. This award is for all of you.

I would like to thank María Cecilia Calzada de Neumann and extend my gratitude to the entire SAP University Alliances team for all the learning throughout this journey. From Victor Fagundes, to Gloria Sandi and Isadora Royer + Ariadne Bessa Rodrigues, as well Prof. Alberto Medeiros for the mentoring, thank you for always keeping us connected and engaged with this inspiring community.

As always, the LAC event was a high-level initiative, bringing together and strengthening a community that is helping shape the future of #education with #technology. The real difference of University Alliances is that it is not only about #software — although we have access to technologies ranging from SAP Business Builders to real SAP applications — but about #people and what we can cocreate together in ways that make pedagogical sense for our reality.

The event also brought important updates on plug-and-play resources designed to enhance student learning and make the adoption of SAP #AI technologies more accessible, practical, and meaningful in the classroom.

The conference was also a valuable opportunity to meet Katharina Schäfer, Global Head of Academic Partnerships at SAP, in person and learn from her perspectives on the future of the UA program, especially regarding strategies for student engagement, portfolio ambidexterity and balancing the #digital transition, faculty engagement in the new platform, and #skills of the future. ☕

On a personal level, mobilizing a University Alliances Program is no easy task. It is time-consuming, but also keeps us connected to cutting-edge concepts and technologies from a #global industry #leader. I am grateful for the opportunity to carry out this mission during this cycle, and hope we can continue building great things together with the UA Community🙏

More to come,

entry.title: New E-learning: Implementing SAP S/4HANA for Advanced Available-to-Promise
entry.content:
Is keeping delivery promises a challenge in your supply chain?

SAP S/4HANA for advanced available-to-promise (ATP), also known as Advanced Available-to-Promise (aATP), is designed to address exactly that. It evaluates supply and demand in real time to ensure accurate and dependable order confirmation dates, while protecting your business priorities and profitability goals. Built natively into the SAP S/4HANA core, it works seamlessly across all deployment options: cloud or on-premise.

Introducing the New E-learning 

Unlock the potential of SAP S/4HANA for advanced ATP with our e-learning:

Implementing SAP S/4HANA for Advanced Available-to-Promise

Through this course, you will:

  • Get comprehensive guidance on implementing SAP S/4HANA for advanced ATP to optimize order promising processes.
  • Explore core aATP capabilities, configure supply protection and product availability checks, and apply best practices for delivering reliable customer commitments in real-time while balancing supply chain constraints and business priorities.
  • Configure and implement SAP S/4HANA for advanced ATP capabilities to deliver accurate order commitments while managing supply chain constraints and business priorities.

Ready to get started? Dive into the e-learning experience now!

 

Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps

Beyond e-learnings, SAP Enterprise Support offers additional resources to help you drive innovation. Value maps on SAP Community bring together guidance, recommended actions, and supporting resources to help you decide what to focus on next - all included in your SAP cloud subscription.

👉  Watch our short demo video, then head over to our page to explore our range of topics and request a call with a topic expert for guidance tailored to your goals.

 

About the author: 
Mabelle Roque Miguel is a Topic Expert for SAP Enterprise Support value maps, specializing in SAP Supply Chain Management solutions. Based in Singapore, Mabelle has been with SAP since 2007, taking on diverse roles that have largely centered around customer engagement and success. Now, her focus is on guiding businesses through their supply chain journey, helping them accelerate the adoption of SAP supply chain management solutions with value map content and SAP Enterprise Support offerings.

  Have questions or feedback around this content? Feel free to leave a comment below or contact @MabelleRM.

entry.title: Re: Strategies for Downtime-Optimized DMO : doDMO, doC, DMOVE2S4 for large databases
entry.content:
Hi @sumitjais 

I would like to get some clarification on a use-case involving DMOV2S4 (Downtime Optimized DMO).

Source Landscape:

Source system: SAP ECC 6.0 EHP7
Database: Oracle 19 (>20 TB)
OS: Oracle Linux 8
Hosting: AWS
Target Scenario:

Conversion to SAP S/4HANA (on-premise deployment model on AWS)
Queries:
Based on recent documentation/blogs, DMOV2S4 with DoDMO is often referenced in the context of RISE with SAP transformations.

Is DMOV2S4 still applicable and supported for large (>20 TB) Oracle-based systems in a standard on-premise S/4HANA conversion scenario (non-RISE, AWS- AWS)
From an architecture perspective:

Is it feasible to provision a target Application Server (AAS) in the new AWS landscape, connect it to the source Oracle DB, and execute SUM from this AAS on target landscape?
During the conversion, will SUM handle:
ASCS instance relocation, and
Transition of the source PAS to the target AAS aligned with the S/4HANA target release?

 

Any guidance, recommendations, or best practices for this scenario would be highly appreciated.
Thank you.

entry.title: Predictive Cutover Intelligence in SAP S/4HANA Programs
entry.content:
Predictive Cutover Intelligence in SAP S/4HANA Programs: Why Traditional Readiness Tracking Is No Longer Enough

Introduction

One thing I’ve noticed across multiple SAP S/4HANA transformation programs is that cutover issues rarely come from a single major failure. Most of the time, problems build gradually across testing cycles, reconciliation activities, integrations, and operational readiness checkpoints.

In one project, the overall migration status looked “green” in weekly meetings, but teams were quietly struggling with repeated interface failures and unresolved reconciliation gaps between regions. Individually, none of the issues looked critical. Together, they created significant pressure during the final cutover preparation phase.

That experience made me realize that traditional readiness tracking often gives leadership a delayed view of project health. By the time problems become visible in status reports, teams are already reacting instead of proactively managing risks.

Over time, I started exploring how predictive analytics and AI-driven monitoring could improve visibility across migration activities and help identify operational risks much earlier in the transformation lifecycle.

Why Traditional Readiness Tracking Becomes a Problem

Most SAP programs still rely heavily on:

  • spreadsheets

  • manual checkpoints

  • fragmented status reporting

  • static governance reviews

These methods work initially, but as transformation programs scale across multiple regions, systems, and workstreams, visibility becomes increasingly difficult.

I’ve seen situations where:

  • testing teams reported progress differently

  • integration risks were tracked separately

  • reconciliation failures were buried inside operational logs

  • leadership received conflicting readiness updates

As a result, teams spent more time consolidating information than solving actual issues.

AI Changes the Timing of Risk Detection

The biggest advantage of predictive monitoring is not automation alone — it’s earlier visibility.

Instead of waiting for failed milestones or escalation meetings, AI-driven monitoring can continuously analyze:

  • reconciliation trends

  • test execution patterns

  • failed background jobs

  • interface instability

  • master data inconsistencies

  • operational readiness metrics

This helps teams identify anomalies before they start affecting cutover timelines.

Traditional Readiness Tracking vs Predictive AI-Driven Monitoring

Illustrative example showing how AI-driven monitoring helps identify migration and operational risks earlier compared to traditional reactive readiness tracking approaches.

A Real Example That Changed My Perspective

During one SAP transformation, a specific financial integration kept failing intermittently during testing cycles. Initially, teams treated it as an isolated technical issue because the failures were inconsistent and spread across different environments.

But after reviewing historical execution trends, we noticed a pattern:
the failures increased whenever transaction volumes crossed certain thresholds during parallel reconciliation activities.

The issue itself was not caused by the interface logic. It was a hidden infrastructure bottleneck that only became visible under combined processing loads.

Without trend analysis, we probably would have discovered it much later during cutover simulation.

Instead, the infrastructure team resolved the bottleneck weeks before go-live preparation started.

That experience showed me how valuable predictive operational visibility can become in large-scale SAP programs.

Building a Predictive Readiness Framework

From my experience, predictive readiness works best when integrated directly into migration governance processes.

The most effective programs combine:

  • migration validation cycles

  • operational monitoring

  • reconciliation analytics

  • testing metrics

  • integration health tracking

  • readiness scoring

into a centralized monitoring framework.

This creates a much more realistic picture of project stability.

AI-Driven Predictive Readiness Monitoring Workflow

Illustrative workflow demonstrating how predictive monitoring integrates data ingestion, anomaly detection, risk analysis, recommendations, and operational follow-up activities during SAP S/4HANA programs.

Executive Visibility During Cutover

One major challenge during cutover periods is decision fatigue.

Leadership teams receive updates from:

  • migration teams

  • testing leads

  • integration teams

  • business users

  • infrastructure teams

  • operations teams

Often, the information is fragmented and difficult to interpret quickly.

Centralized readiness dashboards help simplify this process by providing:

  • real-time readiness scores

  • unresolved risk visibility

  • reconciliation trends

  • integration stability metrics

  • operational alerts

During one program, a centralized readiness dashboard reduced escalation delays significantly because every team was reviewing the same operational metrics instead of maintaining disconnected spreadsheets.

Instead of debating status accuracy, teams focused directly on issue resolution.

Executive Readiness Dashboard for SAP S/4HANA Cutover Monitoring

Illustrative dashboard example showing reconciliation trends, operational readiness scoring, integration stability metrics, and risk visibility during SAP S/4HANA cutover preparation.

Where Predictive Monitoring Creates the Most Value

From what I’ve observed, predictive readiness monitoring provides the strongest value in these areas:

Earlier Escalation Visibility

Recurring issues become visible before they impact critical milestones.

Better Cross-Functional Alignment

All teams work from the same operational view.

Reduced Last-Minute Firefighting

Teams spend less time reacting to surprises close to go-live.

Stronger Governance Discipline

Trend analysis improves accountability and follow-up consistency.

Better Leadership Decisions

Executives can prioritize remediation efforts using real operational signals instead of static reporting.

Important Reality Check

AI will not eliminate the need for experienced SAP teams.

Successful transformations still depend heavily on:

  • strong governance

  • experienced functional consultants

  • disciplined validation cycles

  • operational coordination

  • business engagement

What predictive monitoring improves is visibility, timing, and decision-making.

And in large enterprise transformations, timing often determines whether cutover becomes controlled or chaotic.

Conclusion

From my experience, the most successful SAP S/4HANA programs are not the ones without issues. They are the ones where teams identify risks early, maintain visibility across workstreams, and respond proactively before problems become critical.

Traditional readiness tracking often tells teams what already happened.

Predictive monitoring helps teams understand what may happen next.

That shift from reactive reporting to proactive operational intelligence is where AI can create meaningful value in SAP S/4HANA transformation programs.

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Event Routing per Domain with Metadata-Driven Consumer Orchestration
entry.content:
@venkatamandavilli 

Thank you for the excellent summary. You captured the exact core value: separating the execution engine from the configuration state to achieve true immutability.

However, shifting complexity from hardcoded iFlows to runtime metadata means the Value Mapping effectively becomes the architecture. To prevent metadata chaos, strict governance via SDIA, ODCP, and EDCP is non-negotiable:

  • Domain Ownership: Dedicated Domain Architects must act as gatekeepers to prevent cross-domain pollution.

  • Semantic Discipline: The naming conventions are the foundation. If semantic discipline relaxes, the routing fails.

  • Pure Transport: We freeze the iFlow footprint, forcing SAP CPI to act purely as a predictable transport engine.

In my view, the peak of this paradigm shift is combining GDCR + DDCR (using Key-Value Mapping and JavaScript Policies).

I recently validated this approach in an independent stress test across 5 gateways (SAP, Azure, AWS, Kong, Zuul), a 43-sensor IoT setup (K8s/Istio), and an LLM pipeline across 5 languages. Testing KVM, Redis, and Hash Maps with over 2M concurrent messages, the pattern achieved a 100% success rate (replicate here: https://github.com/rhviana/deip/tree/main/gdcr-proven).

Just as the Smart Router freezes the event layer, GDCR + DDCR destroys Proxy Sprawl and the 1:1 API proliferation trap. The API proxy becomes completely immutable—no redeployments, no policy stitching.

Immutability eliminates deployment overhead, but it shifts the battlefield to governance. Without a structured framework like SDIA, configuration sprawl can become just as dangerous as artifact sprawl.

Appreciate you bringing this critical perspective to the discussion!

Regards,

Viana

entry.title: Re: What is the Use of Private Environment in SAP Build P...
entry.content: A private environment in SAP Build Process Automation is a dedicated, isolated area where you: • Deploy and run projects separately from other teams or environments. • Limit access to specific users and roles, so not everyone in the tenant can see or trigger the same processes. • Organize by domain or business area, for example one environment for Finance automations, one for HR, and one for Procurement. • Keep test and production separate by using different environments, so developers work in one place and users run confirmed processes in another. Example: A large company uses a private environment for Finance to automate invoice approvals and expense workflows. Only the Finance team and a few IT admins have access, and the Finance processes don’t mix with HR or Procurement processes in the same environment. This reduces risk, improves security, and makes maintenance easier.

entry.title: Re: What is the Use of Private Environment in SAP Build P...
entry.content:
A private (shared) environment in SAP Build Process Automation is a dedicated, isolated workspace where you deploy and run projects. It’s mostly used when you want stronger security, better isolation, and more controlled access than the tenant-wide public/default environment allows.

Key purposes

  • Isolation by domain or team
    You can create separate environments for Finance, HR, Procurement, etc., so each team’s processes and resources are logically separated.

  • Better security and access control
    Shared environments let you limit access to specific users and roles, so not everyone in the tenant can see or trigger the same processes.

  • Cleaner version and dependency management
    When a project version is deployed to a shared environment, it’s always the latest active version in that environment, and dependencies within that environment stay consistent.

  • Separate test and production
    You can use different environments for dev/test and production, reducing the risk of accidentally running or modifying processes in the wrong place.

Example

A company uses a private shared environment for Finance to automate invoice approvals and expense workflows. Only the Finance team and a few IT admins have access, and these Finance processes don’t mix with HR or Procurement processes in the same environment. This reduces risk, improves security, and makes maintenance easier.

entry.title: Re: Issues faced in CPM forecasting
entry.content:
In SAP Commercial Project Management (CPM) – Forecasting, the behavior you’re seeing for EAC and ETC is usually due to which forecasting mode you’re using and how the system is configured for EAC calculation.

What CPM supports

According to SAP, CPM supports two main forecasting approaches:

  1. Forecast based on actuals

    • EAC = Actuals from past periods + planned future amounts

    • ETC = planned future amounts only

    • This matches the formula:

      EAC=Actual Cost (AC)+ETC\text{EAC} = \text{Actual Cost (AC)} + \text{ETC}EAC=Actual Cost (AC)+ETC

      productive+2

  2. Forecast where ETC is recalculated based on actuals, but EAC is kept constant

    • In this mode, the system does not automatically set EAC = AC + ETC; EAC may be fixed or calculated differently.

From your description:

After adjusting the quantity, the ETC is calculated correctly based on the remaining plan.

However, the EAC is not summing up the actuals to date + ETC. Instead, it is only showing the ETC value.

This strongly suggests your scenario is using the second forecasting mode (constant EAC or custom EAC logic) instead of Forecast based on actuals.

What to check

To get EAC = Actuals to date + ETC, confirm:

  1. Forecasting mode / method

    • In your forecasting configuration, ensure the method is set to “Forecast based on actuals” (EAC = AC + ETC).

    • Make sure it’s not set to a mode where EAC is kept constant or calculated by a different formula.

  2. Actuals posting

    • Verify that actual costs/revenues are posted correctly and are visible in the relevant periods.

    • If actuals are not recognized by the forecasting run, EAC will look like it only shows ETC.

  3. Custom addition or overrides

    • Check if there’s any custom logic, enhancement, or manual override on EAC that sets it directly to ETC or ignores actuals.

  4. Documentation alignment

    • SAP’s official forecasting documentation describes exactly this behavior and the two modes, so you can compare your setup with the described behavior.

If you’re still seeing EAC = ETC only

If the mode is correctly set to “Forecast based on actuals” and actuals are posted, then this is likely a configuration or enhancement issue and should be opened with SAP (or your internal ABAP/Basis team) as a potential bug or custom code interference.

entry.title: Re: Unable to open MDK Client 25.9.1 in simulator
entry.content:
This looks like a known issue with the MDK branded client 25.9.1 on iOS simulator, especially when using macOS Tahoe (26.x) and Xcode 26.x. SAP has documented a similar crash where the MDK client app fails to launch on simulator due to compatibility issues.userapps.sap+1

What to try

  1. Check Xcode / macOS compatibility

    • SAP notes that MDK client apps can fail to launch on simulator when built with macOS Tahoe 26.x + Xcode 26.x.

    • If possible, try building and running the client on an older, supported macOS/Xcode version that matches the MDK SDK’s recommended environment.

  2. Use device instead of simulator

    • If the simulator is problematic, test the branded client on a real iOS device. This often bypasses simulator-specific issues.

  3. Re-authenticate / restart the client

    • Kill the app in the simulator completely and restart it on the next launch.

    • This forces the client to re-authenticate with SAP Mobile Services and can resolve blank-screen / white-screen issues.

  4. Check logs

    • Enable client logging and review for errors. SAP’s MDK troubleshooting blog suggests using log stream in Terminal and filtering for mdkclient to see what’s failing.

  5. Verify SDK and prerequisites

    • Make sure you’re using the latest MDK SDK and that all prerequisites are installed correctly.

    • Confirm that the build script (./create-client.command or equivalent) completes successfully without errors.

  6. If using a branded client

    • Confirm that the branding configuration and BrandedSettings.json are correct, without typos or invalid characters.

    • If this is a customer-branded client, open a SAP support ticket referencing the iOS simulator crash and the MDK version (25.9.1).

entry.title: Re: Activate the “No Rolling” flag in T-code SNRO for obj...
entry.content:
The “No Rolling” flag in transaction SNRO for number range object RV_BELEG is often greyed out and not editable in production-like systems, especially when the object is already configured as a rolling number range.userapps.sap+1

What “No Rolling” vs “Rolling” means

  • Rolling (flag NOT set):
    When the interval is used up (you reach the “To” number), numbering automatically starts again from the “From” number.

  • No Rolling (flag IS set):
    When the interval is used up, no more numbers can be assigned from that interval. You must add a new interval or increase the “To” number.

Why you can’t activate it

SAP notes and help documentation show that:

  • For some number range objects (especially critical ones like RV_BELEG), the Rolling/No Rolling attribute is locked after initial setup or in certain system types.

  • The flag may be non-editable when the object is already in use or when the system configuration prevents changing this attribute.

What you can do instead

  1. Check if you really need “No Rolling”

    • For RV_BELEG (billing documents), the question is whether you must have gap-free numbering in all cases.

    • If gap-free numbering is required, confirm with the SD team whether:

      • You need to increase the “To” number of the interval, or

      • You need to add a new interval instead of trying to switch to No Rolling.

  2. Increase the “To” number or add a new interval

    • In SNRO, for object RV_BELEG, you can:

      • Edit the existing interval and increase the To number (e.g. from 99999999 to 9999999999), or

      • Add a new interval with a higher range.

    • This is often the supported approach instead of switching to No Rolling.

  3. Verify the “To-year flag” / buffering settings

    • SAP has documented similar issues where the “To-year flag” is greyed out and cannot be changed for RV_BELEG.

    • Buffering and number range settings for RV_BELEG are also controlled by SAP Notes and customizing (e.g., SAP Note 23835).

    • If this is a business requirement, check whether a relevant SAP Note or system-specific configuration is blocking the change.

  4. If it’s a transport-related issue

    • Confirm that:

      • You’re working in the correct client in development.

      • The transport request is included and not locked.

      • There’s no customizing lock or background job affecting the number range object.

  5. Contact SAP / application team

    • If the business absolutely requires No Rolling for RV_BELEG and you cannot change the attribute, you may need to:

      • Open a SAP ticket, or

      • Coordinate with the SD/Billing application team and Basis to confirm the supported approach for your system version.

entry.title: Re: Version Control Made Simple with Application Frontend Service
entry.content: Very helpful. The post makes version control for frontend apps feel much simpler by showing how the version is defined in manifest.json and how you can deploy without activation, test via version-specific URLs, and then promote the new version with a single CLI command or in the BTP Cockpit. I especially like the separation between the main URL (always serves the active version) and version-specific URLs, which makes testing and rollbacks much safer.

entry.title: Re: How to Implement SAP Business One projects successfully
entry.content:
Great breakdown on the implementation steps. Having run 20+ SMB go-lives in the Hyderabad market, I'd add one critical nuance: the go-live week is where Hyderabad SMBs lose the most money — and it's almost never a technical issue.

Three failure patterns I see repeatedly:

1. Master data validation is skipped under time pressure.
Teams migrate from Tally/legacy spreadsheets and the item master is "good enough" on cutover night. Three weeks later, inventory reports are useless because unit of measure conversions weren't standardized. The fix: run a parallel inventory count in the old system AND SAP B1 for 2 weeks before go-live. Any variance >2% stops go-live until resolved.

2. User training is treated as a checkbox.
SMB owners assume the consultant will "handle it." What actually happens: 3 PowerPoint slides, a rushed demo, and a team that can log in but can't process a goods receipt. The result: workarounds that corrupt data, followed by 6 months of cleanup. Better model: identify 2–3 power users per department in Week 1. Train them deeply. They become your go-live support army.

3. No cutover rollback plan.
If something breaks on go-live night, what's the decision tree? Who decides to rollback vs. push through? This sounds obvious but almost no SMB implementation documents this. The result: 48 hours of chaos before the inevitable rollback — which costs more than if you'd planned for it.

The single best investment: A formal 3-day parallel run before go-live where the team processes every real transaction in BOTH systems simultaneously. Painful? Yes. Expensive? Yes. Cheaper than data recovery after a bad cutover? Far less.

One more thing worth calling out for 2025 implementations: the teams that recover fastest from go-live turbulence are the ones with real-time data visibility from Day 1 — not waiting for month-end reports to discover what went wrong. This is where an AI operations layer on top of SAP B1 (we use TekAI internally for our clients) makes a material difference: your finance team sees cashflow and inventory KPIs live, not 3 weeks after cutover when the damage is already done.

What specific module are you struggling with? Happy to go deeper.

— Venkata Siva Reddy Polu, TEKROI
SAP B1 implementation for Hyderabad pharma and manufacturing SMBs | TekAI: AI-powered operations intelligence on SAP B1

entry.title: Re: Build Process Automation navigating SAP GUI to Excel
entry.content:
To automate SAP GUI → Excel with SAP Build Process Automation (BPA), the supported pattern is:

  1. Record the SAP GUI workflow

    • In BPA, use the Windows GUI Recorder to record your SAP transaction (e.g., VA01, VA02, FBL3N, etc.).

    • Ensure SAP GUI scripting is enabled on your workstation and allowed by IT; otherwise the recorder won’t capture properly.

  2. Create environment variables for secrets and paths
    In your BPA project:

    • Create an environment variable for the Excel file path (e.g., excel_file_path).

    • Optionally create one for the password if you’re not using SSO.

  3. Add Excel activities to the automation
    In your automation flow:

    • Add Open Excel Instance before the SAP Logon screen.

    • Add Excel Cloud Link after that, and browse to select your Excel file (or use the excel_file_path environment variable).

    • Create a Data Type from the Excel data (e.g., “Order Details”) so BPA can map columns like Material, Quantity, Customer, etc.

    • Add Close Excel Instance near the end, before terminating the SAP application.

  4. Loop through Excel rows and drive SAP

    • Use a For each control to loop over the Excel rows (returned from Excel Cloud Link).

    • Inside the loop:

      • Map each Excel column to the corresponding SAP field using Set Element and Set Key (for combo boxes).

      • Convert numbers to strings if needed, e.g.:
        Step29.currentMember.orderQuantity.toString()

  5. Extract data from SAP to Excel (reverse direction)
    If you want to read SAP data into Excel:

    • Use Get Visible Rows Count (GUI Table) to determine how many rows are in the SAP table.

    • Loop through rows in SAP, capture values, and write them into Excel via the Excel Cloud Link or Excel activities.developers.

  6. Optional: add conditions and logging

    • Add a Condition activity if you need special logic (e.g., last row processing, error handling).

    • Use Log Message to write status/info to the automation logs.

Alternative approaches

  • VBA + SAP GUI Scripting
    If BPA is not an option, you can use Excel VBA to call SAP GUI scripting directly from Excel (record a script, then embed it in a VBA module).

  • Desktop automation tools
    Tools like Precisely Automate Studio or other RPA solutions can also handle Excel ↔ SAP automation, but BPA is the native SAP approach.

entry.title: Re: The future of data is conversational and agentic AI — Are you ready?
entry.content:
Hi Martin,

I totally agree with you when discussing efficiency. If used effectively, I think we can leverage these AI tools to optimize the repetitive tasks in our lives and/or help us organize our thoughts. I sometimes find that if I have too many ideas at once, I can just drop a "word salad" on a platform and have it find patterns that help "clean up" my thought process.

It reminds me of the saying from Mark Twain, “I didn't have time to write a short letter, so I wrote a long one instead.” I find that where I get the most value out of AI is in, like you said, enhancing my thoughts, but not changing them. 

I also agree with what you said about the building blocks. There's a term in AI called "Grounding", and it is the process of connecting an AI model's abstract knowledge to real-world, verifiable facts or specific data. This anchors the model's responses to certain guardrails you can set up when you know a direct answer, therefore filling it with context.

You mentioned you define steps before using AI tools, do you find they're similar, routine steps every time, or do you make them very specific each time?

entry.title: Building a Procurement Agent for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition with SAP Joule Studio – Part 1
entry.content:
TL;DR: This post is part of a three-part series on building a custom AI agent for Joule, specialized in creating purchase requisitions in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition through a single prompt. In this first part, we walk through the steps to identify and enable APIs for consumption via the SAP Build suite.

 

A few days ago, the blog Adapting SAP Joule for Purchase Requisition Creation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition was published, showing the execution of a custom Joule Agent capable of understanding, in a single prompt, the creation of a purchase requisition in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. This use case was modeled using the SAP Build suite, more specifically Joule Studio, a low-code tool for building AI agents and capabilities in SAP Joule. The goal was to demonstrate that a wide variety of scenarios can be enabled in just a few steps using the tools provided by SAP BTP.

As a follow-up to the previous blog, this post is part of a series of 3 posts in which we describe the process that you and your team can follow to enable custom scenarios in Joule:

  1. In the first part, we walk through the steps to find APIs and enable them for consumption in the SAP Build suite.
  2. In the second part, we show how to build a Joule Skill and what to consider when creating it, all within Joule Studio. We will also build a process to support unit-of-measure mapping using SAP Build Process Automation.
  3. Finally, in the third part, we describe what to consider when building the Joule Agent for procurement.

It is important to note that, here, we are focused on this specific PR-creation use case, but the considerations are general and applicable to other scenarios. We also highlight that this series takes a more technical approach, explaining how to navigate between the tools.

Note: at SAP Sapphire 2026, the new Joule Studio was announced, making agent creation even easier. Stay tuned for more details soon.

 

Business Requirement and Problem Statement

Although a standard use case for PR creation exists, it covers specific scenarios. For example, the document type generated by this capability is always NB, which is not always suited to our customers. It can also be too verbose for certain users, as some back-and-forth messages are required to complete it. For this reason, based on internal discussions and customer feedback, we decided to demonstrate how to extend these scenarios to make them broader and more aligned, including even custom fields.

In our case, here are the requirements:

  1. That the agent be capable of identifying the request and generating the purchase requisition in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition with a single prompt, eliminating the need to go through multiple messages. The idea is to optimize the work of specialists who already know the material code, plant code, and other system information.
  2. That the agent be able to simultaneously handle the header and items in the same prompt, recognizing which fields belonged to the header, how many items there were, and what the values of the respective fields were.
  3. That the agent be capable of identifying, in this prompt, multiple fields, including a custom field called ZJoule, made available through a standard API via key user extensibility (Custom Fields app).

Note: if you are not familiar with the key user extensibility solution for extending the standard fields, we suggest reviewing some content related to the Custom Fields app, such as How to create custom fields in the Business Process with "Custom Field Fiori App ( ID F1481)", Create Custom fields for Business Partner SAP Fiori screens in SAP S/4HANA and Adding Field in standard Fiori apps of S/4HANA with Custom Fields and Logic.

In our example, these requirements will be addressed on an SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025 FPS01 system.

 

Before you Begin

This is a scenario that requires the involvement of multiple professional roles: system administrators, developers, and functional professionals. It is not a one-person guide, so at the beginning of each section, you will find the roles required to proceed to the next step.

Regarding the technical requirements, the following are needed: an SAP BTP subaccount with Cloud Foundry runtime enabled, an active SAP Build suite subscription (with SAP Build Process Automation and SAP Joule Studio), an SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system, a connection between this system and the SAP BTP subaccount, and a Joule service subscription. The initial provisioning configurations for these services must also be in place.

Note: Be aware of the supported data centers by Joule Studio to ensure access to the service via SAP Build.

Please note that the general purpose of this blog series is to serve as a guide presenting the steps to build your custom Joule Agent scenario, demonstrating, through an example, how you can attempt to run it. It is not, however, intended as a step-by-step tutorial, which means some prior knowledge is expected from the professionals who will execute each step.

 

Selecting APIs for Your Business Requirement

Roles: functional consultants and developers.

Before any AI agent instructions or configurations, it is necessary to spend some time understanding how your scenario will be enabled for external consumption.  The Joule Agent is a component that runs on top of the Joule service on SAP Business Technology Platform and, in some way, needs to connect to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition to create the purchase requisition.

A very useful tool for finding this approach is the SAP Business Accelerator Hub, a catalog where all remote SAP APIs are published and documented. Here you will find everything you need to know about these connectors:

  • Purpose
  • Specification in various formats (OpenAPI, EDMX, SOAP, etc.)
  • Supported authentication methods
  • Configuration details
  • Extensibility
  • Product and release
  • Reference for each operation
  • Code samples for major programming languages
  • Documentation and additional help pages.

Here you can explore the various APIs, reviewing their purpose and their ability to meet your business requirements.

Finding the purchase requisition API via SAP API Business Hub

Note: Use the APIs in accordance with their purpose, as described in the official documentation. In case of further doubts, review the API Policy.

 

In this use case scenario, we will use the OData V4 API API_PURCHASEREQUISITION_2 for Purchase Requisition. It can be found in the catalog by searching for the product SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and filtering by "Purchase requisitions". The technical name of the service group can be found in the Overview tab, under the API Resources section.

Note that an OData V2 version exists but is already marked as "Deprecated". Therefore, it is not recommended for new developments.

Note: always make sure to select the version that corresponds to your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition to ensure the API is available in your system. Use the "Select Release" dropdown to specify it.

Still in API Resources, you can find the path to call the service from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. This path will be used later when we configure the destination in SAP BTP.

Configuration details - Purchase Requisition (OData V4 API)

 

In the API Reference section, you can browse the various objects that can be read and manipulated by these APIs. When you select one of the objects, you will see the available operations, as well as the fields required for their execution. There is also the response structure, which shows which fields will be returned upon successful execution of the service. This view is particularly useful for functional colleagues, who can map names to fields found in SAP S/4HANA business objects.

Reviewing the objects and operations in SAP Business Accelerator Hub

 

For our scenario, we will use the Purchase Requisition object, with a POST operation on the OData entity /PurchaseReqn. This operation allows you to create a purchase requisition with all the required fields, including a structure to add items in the same call.

A characteristic of this API is the need to pass the base unit in ISO format (SAP note 3525094). Since this is not a commercial unit of measure code (the most commonly used), a second API will be required to retrieve it based on the material IDs indicated in the prompt. For this, we will use the API_PRODUCT API for Products, with a GET operation on the OData entity /Product.

 

Activating the API in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

Roles: system administrators.

After locating the API to be used, you must ensure that it is activated in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system. Depending on the type of API chosen, OData V2 or OData V4, different steps must be followed.

For OData V2 APIs, you can follow the documentation Activate and Maintain Services. There you will find details about the features of transaction /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE and how to use it.

For OData V4 APIs, you can follow the documentation available in Service Group Publishing. This help page details the steps to publish and configure a service using transaction /IWFND/V4_ADMIN.

Using transaction /IWFND/V4_ADMIN to publish an OData V4 API

In our example, we used transaction /IWFND/V4_ADMIN to publish the service group API_PURCHASEREQUISITION_2. Keep in mind that your system may have different configurations for system alias.

After publishing the service, you can also test it using the SAP Gateway Client (transaction /IWFND/GW_CLIENT) or with any HTTP client of your choice, such as Postman, Insomnia, or Bruno.

 

Creating a Destination in SAP BTP

Roles: system administrators.

Destinations are abstractions created to mask or hide addresses, keys, certificates, and other sensitive information. By using them, it is not necessary to store this information in your developments. As a best practice, let’s create a destination in SAP BTP to expose the URL of the newly published service. These activities are typically performed by system administrators, and the generated destinations are then shared with colleagues so they can proceed.

Before executing this step, your system must already be correctly exposed to SAP Business Technology Platform through a SAP Cloud Connector. You can check which systems are already exposed to SAP BTP in SAP BTP Cockpit, under Connectivity > Cloud Connectors.

Available back-end systems via Cloud Connector in a location ID

 

To create the destination in SAP BTP Cockpit, go to the Connectivity > Destinations section and click the Create button. In the popup, select From Scratch. Then, fill in the fields according to the table below:

Field name

Value

Name

<name of your destination>

Type

HTTP

Description

<brief description of the destination's purpose>

Proxy Type

OnPremise

URL

<API URL from API Business Hub. Replace the server and port with the corresponding values of the exposed back-end system>

Location ID

<location ID used to expose the back-end system>

Authentication

BasicAuthentication or PrincipalPropagation (productive scenario)

User

<your user ID in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition>

Password

<your login password for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition>

Additional Properties

Key

Value

sap-client

300

sap.applicationdevelopment.actions.enabled

true

sap.processautomation.enabled

true

sap.build.usage

odata_gen

 

In our example, to expose the URL we found for Purchase Requisition, we used the following information:

Field name

Value

Name

EasyPurchaseRequisitionV4

Type

HTTP

Description

Easy Purchase Requisition on Joule

Proxy Type

OnPremise

URL

http://<server>:<port>/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_purchaserequisition_2/srvd_a2x/sap/purchaserequisition/0001/

Authentication

BasicAuthentication

User

<user>

Password

<password>

Additional Properties

Key

Value

sap-client

300

sap.applicationdevelopment.actions.enabled

true

sap.processautomation.enabled

true

sap.build.usage

odata_gen

Purchase Requisition Destination in SAP BTP

 

For exposing the URL for Products, we used the following information:

Field name

Value

Name

EPRProductV4

Type

HTTP

Description

Easy Purchase Requisition on Joule - Products

Proxy Type

OnPremise

URL

http://<server>:<port>/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_product/srvd_a2x/sap/product/0002/

Authentication

BasicAuthentication

User

<user>

Password

<password>

Additional Properties

Key

Value

sap-client

<SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition client ID>

sap.applicationdevelopment.actions.enabled

true

sap.processautomation.enabled

true

sap.build.usage

odata_gen

 

For further questions about destination management in SAP BTP, refer to the documentation  Using the Destinations Editor in the Cockpit .

 

Creating an Environment in SAP Build

Roles: system administrators.

An environment is a functional area in which you deploy and run your SAP Build Process Automation and SAP Joule projects. It is a prerequisite for running your Joule Agent.

To create an environment, from the SAP Build Lobby, navigate to the Control Tower > Environments. Then, click Create and provide a name, an ID, and a description for your environment.

Creating an Environment in SAP Build

 

For this example, we created the environment Easy Purchase Requisition. This object will be used in part 2.

 

Enabling Destinations in SAP Build

Roles: system administrators.

A second step is required for destinations created in SAP BTP Cockpit to become visible in SAP Build. After accessing the SAP Build Lobby, go to Control Tower > Destinations.

Clicking Add opens a pop-up window that lets you select the destination. Search for the destination you created and select it. Next, choose the environment in which you want the destination to be available. You can select specific environments or make it available to all environments at once. If you prefer a more granular selection, make sure the environment you created is selected.

More information on how to manage destinations in SAP Build can be found at Manage Destinations.

Maintaining Destinations in SAP Build

 

For the purchase requisition and product scenarios, we selected, respectively, the destinations EasyPurchaseRequisitionV4 and EPRProductV4 and enabled them for consumption across all environments, without any restrictions.

 

Creating Actions in SAP Build

Roles: functional consultants and developers.

We have now reached the last step of our first part: creating Actions. An Action is a connector that encapsulates one or more APIs. It is the artifact that will later be invoked by the Joule Skill and the Process Automation we will build.

To create an action, you must first create a dedicated project. There are essentially two ways to obtain API contracts:

  • Via Live API, using SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) service, ABAP Restful Application Programming Model (RAP) service, generic OData destinations, a system available through the Unified Customer Landscape, SAP Integration Suite APIs, or RFCs.
  • Via static API specification, using definitions from the API Business Hub, uploading a specification, or creating a specification from scratch.

For this example, we will use the Live APIs from OData destinations.

 

From the SAP Build Lobby, in the Connectors section, click Actions to view all existing projects created to expose APIs for SAP Build developments. Click Create to open the pop-up window and choose your data source. Select OData Destinations. In the list, select the destination you published (e.g., EPRProductV4 or EasyPurchaseRequisitionV4).

If the configuration is correct, the service metadata will be loaded automatically, displaying all available operations from the selected APIs. This content is informational only and will be used shortly during modeling. Next, specify a name for your project, a brief description of the project, and the APIs that will be encapsulated.

Creating an Action project from an OData Destination

Note: if you cannot find your API in the list, verify that the parameter "sap.build.usage" is set to "odata_gen" in your destination configuration in SAP BTP Cockpit.

 

In our procurement example, two projects were created:

  • Easy Purchase Requisition - APIs v4 – project to encapsulate the API related to purchase requisition creation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.
  • Easy Purchase Requisition – Product APIs v4 – project to encapsulate the APIs for material lookup in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.

 

You will be redirected to the Actions modeler. From the API contract, select the operations identified during the requirements phase. There is a limit of 20 operations per project, so avoid selecting unnecessary operations.

Selecting objects and operations from the API contract

 

 

 

For this example, we selected the POST operation for the entity /PurchaseReqn (Add new entity to PurchaseReqn).

After finalizing the selection, click Add. Then, you can now navigate through the structure of the selected operations.

In the Input tab, you will find the parameters and, when applicable, the request body required to make the call, including the navigations. You can make adjustments to data types and the mandatory status of fields.

The Input section of operation "Add new entity to PurchaseReqn"

 

Important: every Action containing a POST, PATCH, PUT, or DELETE operation (i.e., one that results in the creation, update, or deletion of data) targeting the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system must have the CSRF token enabled. This is a mechanism to prevent malicious attacks known as Cross Site Request Forgery.

SAP Build includes a CSRF token fetch mechanism, however it is disabled by default. To enable it, click the gear icon in the top-right corner. Click the toggle switch to activate the mechanism. In the endpoint field, add the path "/".

In the Output tab, you will find the response structures for success (2xx) and error (4xx or 5xx) scenarios.

The Output section of operation "Add new entity to PurchaseReqn"

 

In the Test tab, you can test the connectivity and execution of your Action. Select the destination corresponding to the action you want to execute and fill in the form with test data. Before proceeding to the next steps, it is important to ensure the API is working correctly. Be sure to test it so that, if there are integration failures, you know there are no connectivity issues and that your scenario was validated through a direct call.

Testing the Purchase Requisition API

 

In our example, we made a call to create the purchase requisition, validating connectivity and the minimum required values for the operation.

 

Testing the Product API

 

 We also ran connectivity and consumption tests for the product API, validating the execution using a filter and checking the return of the corresponding ISO unit of measure.

 

After completing the required validations, you must Release the Action, followed by its publication. Do not forget this step. Without publishing, your Action will not be visible in the library, making it impossible to use it in the subsequent steps.

Releasing and Publishing the Action

Key Takeaways

After going through all these steps, keep in mind the following points:

  1. Have a use case or scenario well discussed and defined before starting your API investigation. It will allow you to validate the best API option and build the new capability concisely and quickly. Prefer to start with smaller, quick-to-implement cases (quick wins).
  2. A multidisciplinary team is extremely valuable when working with generative AI agents and capabilities. You may have noticed that many of the steps in this process are shared by more than one professional role. Cross-team integration is a key success factor for deploying custom scenarios.
  3. Use the API Business Hub as your primary source for discovering public and remote APIs for S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, paying attention to your system’s release. Also, ensure that the API usage is in compliance with its purpose. Then, enable the API in S/4HANA and run basic tests before investing more time in subsequent steps.
  4. SAP Build requires a series of steps before an API can be consumed:
    1. A destination must be created in SAP BTP Cockpit.
    2. An environment must be created in SAP Build.
    3. The destination must be published for consumption in the SAP Build environment.
  5. An Action is managed within a project. Do not forget to publish it. Without publishing, the action will not be visible in subsequent steps.

 

Next Steps

In the next post, we will cover the creation of the Joule Skill. There, we will use the artifacts from this guide to allow the Joule Agent to perform the desired operation. See you there!

 

Thanks,
Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG Team

entry.title: Re: Issues when using BAdI SD_BIL_DATA_TRANSFER
entry.content:
Hi @MichaelNielsen,

This will not work for you, even though you might see there is a referencesddocument and referencesddocumentitem, but they are not yet persisted, just in memory, and even the i_projectbillingelemententrflw  is still not saved.

Try to find another way, and you can usually only depend on the input, so you might not even see the billing quantities of other items.

It might be an option to save your temporary values in a custom table.

Thanks,

Arafat

entry.title: Onboarding End-user Challenges
entry.content:
Hello,

Our company has been using Onboarding for about nine months, and we continue to receive feedback from a small percent of new hires who experience issues when entering their personal data. Examples include: the address state field not accepting 'Wisconsin,' SSN/National ID entries being rejected even when formatted with dashes, submit buttons not responding, and occasional system timeouts.

While most users complete their tasks without issue, approximately 10% are encountering significant problems that prevent them from finishing the onboarding process and we revert to paper forms (mostly for rehires but this is a whole separate topic). I am desperately trying to identify the root cause. At this point, I am beginning to wonder if the device type may be a contributing factor, as system configuration does not appear to be the issue.

Has anyone observed differences in performance between Android and iOS/Apple devices? Could these issues be related to device or app updates, different browsers or settings, or the need to clear cookies/cache? Additionally, are there any known best practices or common troubleshooting steps that can be communicated proactively to help reduce these user challenges? Such as if you are experiencing issues on your mobile device, try using a home desktop computer.

I would appreciate any insights on how others are addressing or communicating around these types of issues.

Thank you in advance for your suggestions!

~Julie

entry.title: Item 002 Order XXXX budget exceeded, error message BP604
entry.content:
Dear Experts

I have seen many threads regarding the error message BP604 but none of the solutions given have not worked for me. I created and Internal order with investment measure and entered a budget via KO22. Eg USD 5000. I created a PO for USD 4000 (account assignment category F) with the internal order created earlier and GRNed. USD 4000 got assigned to the internal order budget. When I was trying to settle the internal order to AUC via KO88, the error message BP604 budget exceeded message was received. When supplemented the budget I could do the settlement but the report says USD 8000 has been consumed which is incorrect. Budget supplement is not a solution. Also I came across suggestions saying to remove account assignment for the respective transaction types in ACSET. But then I get the error message - Line item 002 profit center not filled.

Any idea how this could be resolved?

Thanks

Kera

entry.title: Re: You Asked for Simpler. SAP Signavio Listened. Here Is What Changes.
entry.content: Where is the functionality for process insights that allowed us to limit what users can see security wise? Limiting based on things such as company codes, sales orgs, etc..  Also, is there any documentation that would help understand the changes related to security?

entry.title: Re: Item 002 Order XXXX budget exceeded, error message BP604
entry.content:
It appears that the PO commitment is already consuming the investment budget, while the KO88 settlement posting to the AUC is also configured as budget-relevant, potentially leading to double budget consumption.

In Customizing ( SPRO) , please review the following configuration under:

Controlling
 → Internal Orders
   → Budgeting and Availability Control

Please verify in particular:

  • Budget Profile
  • Tolerance Limits
  • Assigned Value Categories

Also check which value types are included in Availability Control and budget consumption calculations, especially:

  • Commitments / Actuals
  • Settlement postings

The issue may stem from both the commitment and the settlement being considered simultaneously as budget-consuming values.

entry.title: Re: RE-FX Activation of leases asset with start date in prior year
entry.content: How do you create an asset through the RE-FX contract? I only know how to create the asset using AS01 and then enter the asset in the Valuation Parameters under "Asset".

entry.title: Issue after an ECC to SAP S/4HANA conversion regarding Predictive Accounting / Predictive Commitment
entry.content:
We are facing an issue after an ECC to SAP S/4HANA conversion regarding Predictive Accounting / Predictive Commitments.

In a brownfield conversion scenario, the standard predictive ledger 0E is not automatically created. Therefore, we manually created a predictive ledger (ZE).

However, after the creation of ledger ZE, only newly created or modified purchasing documents generate/update predictive postings in this ledger.

The problem is related to existing Purchase Orders and Purchase Requisitions that were already open before the predictive ledger activation.

When an existing PO or PR is edited, the system behaves as if predictive postings already existed for the original document values. As a result, the system creates:

  • one line reversing the “old” value;
  • one line posting the “new” value.

Since there was never an initial predictive posting for the original document, the final committed amount becomes either:

  • zero;
  • or only the delta between old and new values.

This creates a major inconsistency in the predictive commitments.

To avoid this issue, we would need to initially populate/rebuild the predictive ledger with all existing open Purchase Orders and Purchase Requisitions.

Question:
How can this initial population/rebuild be done?

So far, I could not find any SAP Note or standard report that solves this issue. The closest object I found was report FINS_PRED_REPOST, but it seems to handle only sales documents.

Has anyone experienced this scenario?
Any suggestions, SAP Notes, reports, or workaround ideas would be greatly appreciated.

Regards,
Marcio Blos

#PS #Commitments #PredictiveAccounting #PredictiveCommitment #brownfield #0E #ZE #ProjectSystem

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA DRC: Extending the Canada GST/HST Report to Enable Optional Tax Lines (90, 91, 102).
entry.content:
Introduction

If your organization files a GST/HST return with the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA), you are likely familiar with the standard lines on Form GST34 (the amounts you collect, the credits you claim, and the net tax you remit or recover). While most businesses complete the core required lines and submit, the CRA form also includes optional revenue classification lines (Lines 90, 91, and 102) that allow businesses to break down their total revenue into more specific categories. These lines are used by organizations that deal with a mix of supply types, such as: 

  • Standard taxable sales (e.g., most goods and services subject to GST/HST)
  • Zero-rated supplies (e.g., basic groceries, prescription drugs, exports)
  • Exempt supplies (e.g., residential rents, financial services)
  • Other revenue (e.g., goodwill, sale of capital real property, supplies made outside Canada)

 While these lines are optional for many businesses, some organizations, particularly those in financial services, real estate, or with cross-border operations, may need or choose to complete them.

This blog provides a comprehensive guide to configuring these three additional tax boxes to the standard Canada GST/HST DRC report using the DRC extensibility framework. The process does not require any ABAP development, as all steps are accomplished within the scope of Key User extensibility, thereby ensuring the system remains fully upgrade-safe.

Background: Standard Report Architecture

The standard Canada GST/HST report in SAP DRC is built on the following components:

Component

Value

Report Category (RC)

CA_GST_HST_RITC_RETURN

Report Definition (RD)

CA_GST_REP_V2

The report relies on two key DRC queries:

1. BOX_SUMM: This query:

  • Reads all posted tax documents and their tax box mappings
  • Summarizes tax code values (tax amount and base amount) against their respective tax boxes
  • Prepares source data for the following standard tax boxes: GST_101, GST_103, GST_104, GST_106, GST_107, GST_110, GST_111, GST_135, GST_136, GST_205, GST_405

2. DERIVED_FIELDS: This query applies formula-based calculations on top of the BOX_SUMM output, using ABAP class CL_FICA_VAT_ACR_DERIVEDFIELDS, method GST_REP

Derived Box

Formula

Description

GST_105

GST_103 + GST_104

Total GST/HST collected and adjustments

GST_108

GST_106 + GST_107

Total input tax credits and adjustments

GST_109

GST_105 − GST_108

Net tax

GST_112

GST_110 + GST_111

Total other credits

GST_113A

GST_109 − GST_112

Balance (refund or amount owing)

GST_113B

GST_205 + GST_405

Total other debits

GST_114 / GST_115

Larger of GST_113A / GST_113B

Refund or remittance line

 Note on DERIVED_FIELDS: No changes are required to the DERIVED_FIELDS query. Boxes 90, 91, and 102 are direct summary (source) boxes — their values are derived purely from tax code mappings and carry no formula dependency with other boxes. This significantly simplifies the extension.

 

What Needs to Change

To add support for tax boxes 090, 091, and 102, the following areas require configuration and extension:

  1. Create new tax codes (if required)
  2. Tax box mapping
  3. DRC Query extension
  4. DRC Output schema extension

Step 1 — Create Tax Codes

Configure Your Solution → Finance → Tax on Sales/Purchases → Tax Codes → Define Tax Codes for Sales and Purchases (SSCUI 101016)

Create one tax code for each box: GST_090, GST_091, and GST_102. Use a consistent naming convention that clearly identifies the purpose of each code. Example names: S3 (→ GST_090), S4 (→ GST_091), S5 (→ GST_102).

Setting

Value

Country

CA

Tax Procedure

TAXCAJ

Tax Jurisdiction

CA00 (Federal Level for GST)

Account Key

MWS (Output Tax)

Tax Rate

5% for taxable supplies (Line 90)  |  0% for zero-rated (Line 91)  |  Confirm with tax team for Line 102

 Tax Rate Note: The correct rate depends on how your organization uses these boxes. For zero-rated supply classification (Line 91), use 0%. For standard taxable supply reporting (Line 90), use the applicable GST rate (5%) or HST rate (13%–15% depending on province). For Line 102 (other revenue types such as capital real property or financial services), consult your tax team. Using the wrong rate will cause incorrect calculations on posted documents.

Fig 1 — Tax code creation: Country CA, Jurisdiction CA00, Tax Procedure TAXCAJ

Fig 2 — Tax Rates: Account Key MWS, 5% rate

 Step 2 — Configure Tax Box Mapping

Configure Your Solution  →  Finance  →  Statutory Reporting  →  Country/Region-Specific Settings  →  Canada  →  Generating GST/HST Report  →  Maintain Tax Box Structures (SSCUI 102273)

Select Tax Box Structure Type: 1 (GST/HST).

2a — Tax Groups

Add entries for GST_090, GST_091, and GST_102, assigning the new tax codes (S3/S4/S5) with account key MWS.

 

Fig 3 — Tax Groups: GST_090 → S3 MWS, GST_091 → S4 MWS, GST_102 → S5 MWS

Tax Rate Note: Update Box 101 (mandatory — do not skip). Add tax codes S3, S4, and S5 to the existing Box 101 tax group mapping. Boxes 090, 091, 102 are detail lines; their amounts must roll up into the mandatory Line 101 total. Skipping this will understate total revenue on the CRA return.

2b — Tax Boxes

Add entries for 090, 091, and 102. Set Tax Box OT = Tax Base (these are revenue classification lines, not tax amount lines. This distinguishes them from Lines 103/104, which carry Tax Amount.

Fig 4 — Tax Boxes: GST_090, GST_091, GST_102 with Tax Base output type

2c — Assign Tax Groups to Tax Boxes

One-to-one assignment: GST_090 → GST_090, GST_091 → GST_091, GST_102 → GST_102.

Fig 5 — Assignment table: new tax groups mapped to their tax boxes

Step 3 — Extend the Report Definition

Fiori App: Define Statutory Reports

  • Select Report Category CA_GST_HST_RITC_RETURN / Report Definition CA_GST_REP_V2
  • Click Extend → provide a new report definition name (e.g., ZCA_GSTBOX9091)

All subsequent steps are performed inside the extended report definition. CA_GST_REP_V2 is never modified.

Fig 6 — Extending CA_GST_REP_V2 to ZCA_GSTBOX9091 in Define Statutory Reports 

Step 4 — Add Conditions to BOX_SUMM Query

Within the extended report definition → BOX_SUMM query → Conditions tab.

Field

GST_090

GST_091

GST_102

Condition ID

GST_090

GST_091

GST_102

Field

TaxBox

TaxBox

TaxBox

Type

Literal

Literal

Literal

Value

GST_090

GST_091

GST_102

 

Fig 7 — BOX_SUMM Conditions: GST_090, GST_091, GST_102 added alongside existing conditions

Fig 8 — Condition definition for GST_090: TaxBox / Literal / GST_090

 

Step 5 — Update DRC Output Schema

5a — Export schema from CA_GST_RITC_DOC

Open the standard document CA_GST_RITC_DOC → click Export Schema to download CA_GST_DOC-CA_GST.XSD.

Fig 9 — CA_GST_RITC_DOC: Export Schema entry point

Fig 10 — Export Schema button

5b — Edit the XSD

Insert three new xs:element entries — DEBITS_BOX_090, DEBITS_BOX_091, DEBITS_BOX_102 — after DEBITS_REBATES_405 and before DEBITS_BALANCE_113B. Use type="WERTV12" matching the existing element pattern.

Fig 11 — Original XSD: insertion point after DEBITS_REBATES_405

Fig 12 — Updated XSD: three new elements added

5c — Create a new ALV document and import the schema

In the extended report definition: create a new Document (type: ALV) → import the updated XSD.

Fig 13 — New ALV document: Edit Schema dialog with updated schema imported

5d — Map new elements to BOX_SUM                                                                                           

Map each new schema element using the settings below. The condition filter determines which tax box condition to read from the extended BOX_SUMM query (BOX_SUM).

Schema Element

Data Source

Corresponding Value

Condition

DEBITS_BOX_090

Query Column

BOX_SUM-CALCULATEDTAXBASEAMTINRPTGCRCY

GST_090

DEBITS_BOX_091

Query Column

BOX_SUM-CALCULATEDTAXBASEAMTINRPTGCRCY

GST_091

DEBITS_BOX_102

Query Column

BOX_SUM-CALCULATEDTAXBASEAMTINRPTGCRCY

GST_102

Fig 14 — Mapping table: three new elements with Query Column data source

 

Fig 15 — Select Query: BOX_SUM selected as data source

Fig 16 — Completed mapping: all three boxes mapped to BOX_SUM-CALCULATEDTAXBASEAMTINRPTGCRCY

Fig 17 — Condition assignment for DEBITS_BOX_090 → GST_090

Fig 18 — Condition list: GST_090 → Box90, GST_091 → Box091, GST_102 → Box102 

Step 6 — Assign and Activate

  • Assign the extended report definition to RC CA_GST_HST_RITC_RETURN
  • Assign the reporting category to the reporting entity/company code

Run the report in the Run Reports Fiori app to validate.

Fig 19 — Final report preview: Tax Box 90 shows 1,904.76, confirming correct end-to-end configuration

 

Troubleshooting

Symptom

Likely Cause

Fix

Boxes 090/091/102 show zero

Tax codes not mapped in VG_CUST / SSCUI 102273, or wrong codes used on postings

Verify tax group mapping; check posted documents for the correct tax code

Line 101 understated

New tax codes not added to Box 101 mapping (Step 2d skipped)

Add S3/S4/S5 to Box 101 tax group; re-run report

New lines not visible in the output

XSD not imported, elements missing, or wrong report definition assigned

Re-import updated XSD; verify reporting entity assignment

BOX_SUMM field error on run

GST_090/091/102 conditions missing or contain a typo (case-sensitive)

Check the Conditions tab in the BOX_SUMM query in the extended report definition

Extension missing after transport/promotion

XSD was not manually re-imported into the target system

Manually import the updated XSD in each target system

SSCUI 102273 not visible (PCE only)

User lacks the Key User role, or the Finance configuration scope is not active

Confirm SAP_BR_BPC_EXPERT role and Finance scope in Configure Your Solution

Double-counting on return

Existing tax codes inadvertently mapped to new boxes

Ensure only S3/S4/S5 are assigned to boxes 090/091/102 in VG_CUST / SSCUI 102273

   

Related Resources

  • SAP Help: Document and Reporting Compliance — Canada GST/HST Report
  • SAP Help: Extensibility and Reuse in Statutory Reporting
  • CRA Form GST34: GST/HST Return for Registrants
  • SAP S/4HANA Extensibility – Simplified Guide
  • Localization as a Self-Service for SAP S/4HANA Cloud

entry.title: Re: Materials are getting skipped during MRP live(MD01N) run
entry.content:
Hello

Unless you are marking the regenerative planning flag, MRP will only plan those materials for which the planning file was set, that means, materials for which a planning relevant change has been posted since the previous MRP run. If a material was not planned by MRP, I suggest you to check if the planning file flag is set in MD21. If it's not, this material probably does not have to be planned. 

If the material should be planned by MRP,  then I suggest you to check for any error in transaction MD_MRP_FORCE_CLASSIC or app Display MRP Master Data Issues.

Best regards,
Caetano

entry.title: Re: Uses of Classic MRP : T code - MD_MRP_FORCE_CLASSIC
entry.content: This transaction was designed to be used with MRP Live. Here you can choose whether a material will be planned by MRP Live in ABAP or HANA.
